perturbation.original_text:
Receiving Party shall:
(i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for five (5) years following the date that Disclosing Party first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and 2(c) of this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text:
Receiving Party shall:
(i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for one (1) year following the date that Disclosing Party first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and 2(c) of this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality period from five years to one year could be considered unreasonably short, especially considering the nature of the confidential information. This can be seen as a violation of trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by various U.S. states)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UTSA doesn't specify a minimum confidentiality period, reducing it to one year may not provide sufficient protection, potentially enabling the misappropriation of trade secrets. The law aims to protect against the disclosure and use of trade secrets, and a short confidentiality period weakens this protection.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The change from a five-year to a one-year confidentiality period introduces an inconsistency that undermines the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating the intent of trade secret laws. While 18 USC 1836 doesn't specify a minimum period, it focuses on protecting trade secrets from misappropriation through civil actions. Reducing the confidentiality timeframe significantly weakens this protection, making it easier for misappropriation to occur without legal recourse, directly impacting the trade secret owner's ability to safeguard their information as intended by the law. The original timeframe likely considered a 'reasonable' amount of time to protect the trade secret, while the modification is unreasonably short.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees and consultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party within 30 days upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees and consultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification of a data breach for 30 days directly contradicts data breach notification laws that require immediate or prompt notification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Various State Data Breach Notification Laws
perturbation.law_citation: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCPA mandates businesses to implement reasonable security procedures and practices. Delaying the notification of a data breach by 30 days after discovery violates the spirit and intent of laws requiring prompt notification to affected individuals and regulatory bodies.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 30-day delay in notifying the Disclosing Party of a data breach. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this contradicts data breach notification laws requiring immediate or prompt notification. The Law Explanation also states that this delay violates the spirit and intent of laws requiring prompt notification. Snippet 2, regarding the CCPA, reinforces the importance of responding to consumer requests and giving notices explaining privacy practices. While Snippet 2 doesn't explicitly mention breach notification timelines, the emphasis on consumer rights and business responsibilities implies a need for timely action, which the 30-day delay undermines.

perturbation.original_text: If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees and costs regardless of which party prevails.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the provision for the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees and costs contradicts the general principle that the losing party should bear these costs, particularly in cases of bad faith or willful breach of contract. It might discourage parties from pursuing legitimate claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: American Rule (deviation could be problematic depending on jurisdiction)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the 'American Rule' generally states that each party pays its own legal fees, contracts can shift this responsibility to the losing party. By mandating that each party always bears its costs, the modified term contradicts the possibility of fee-shifting statutes or common law exceptions that aim to deter frivolous litigation or penalize bad faith conduct.

Docket fees and costs of briefs (a) Attorney's and proctor's docket fees in courts of the United States may be taxed as costs as follows: $20 on trial or final hearing (including a default judgment whether entered by the court or by the clerk) in civil, criminal, or admiralty cases, except that in cases of admiralty and maritime jurisdiction where the libellant recovers less than $50 the proctor's docket fee sha
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees and costs. The changed text explicitly states each party shall bear its own fees regardless of who prevails, removing the possibility of fee shifting. The Law Explanation suggests this change contradicts potential fee-shifting statutes or common law exceptions designed to deter frivolous litigation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 28 USC 1923, discusses attorney's docket fees being taxed as costs. While this snippet doesn't mandate fee-shifting, it acknowledges the legal framework where attorney fees can be considered as costs, implying a legal basis for the original text's fee-shifting provision and supporting the contradiction created by the changed text's removal of that provision.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 90 days to 30 days may be insufficient for the other party to adequately protect its interests, especially regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While 30 days might be facially valid, if it demonstrably prejudices one party's ability to protect legitimately held trade secrets or unwind complex arrangements that relied on the NDA, it could be argued the shortened notice period violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contracts. The reasonableness depends on the specific context and potential harm to the disadvantaged party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: duty of good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you duty of good faith The duty of good faith is the principle that directors and officers of a corporation who are making decisions in their capacities as corporate fiduciaries , must act with a conscious regard for their responsibilities in that role. A violation of the duty of good faith may include an intentional neglect of the usual duties of a director or officer, intentionally acting for a purpose other than the benefit of the corporation, or intentionally violating the law. Though there is no private shareholder right of action for a violation of the duty of good faith, its violation may also raise a claim under the duty of loyalty . In Heritage Surveyors & Eng'rs, Inc. v. Nat'l Penn Bank, 801 A.2d 1248 , the court held that “the duty of good faith has been defined as honesty in fact in the conduct or transaction concerned”. Furthermore, the Uniform Commercial Code section 1-304 provides that “Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.” [Last reviewed in September of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords llcs-corporations-partnerships Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business law corporations wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a 90-day notice period for termination, whereas the changed contract reduces this to 30 days. The perturbation explanation notes that this reduction could prejudice one party's ability to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation expands on this, stating that such a shortened period could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing if it harms the disadvantaged party's ability to protect trade secrets or unwind complex arrangements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines the duty of good faith as 'honesty in fact in the conduct or transaction concerned' and states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Thus, the shortened notice period potentially contradicts the duty of good faith, making the change a legally significant contradiction.
